items.q: What does the cosmological argument try to prove?
items.correct: That the existence of the universe requires a first cause or necessary being — usually identified with God — to explain why anything exists at all.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument proves who created life on Earth.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument proves the Big Bang happened.
items.why: Students mix up cosmology (science) with the cosmological argument (philosophy). The argument is about why anything exists at all, not the specific origins of life or the Big Bang.

items.q: What does the teleological (design) argument claim?
items.correct: Apparent purpose, order or complexity in the universe implies an intelligent designer — Paley's watchmaker analogy is the classic example.
items.misconception: The design argument proves God created the world in seven days.
items.random_wrong: The design argument proves evolution is false.
items.why: The argument is philosophical, not anti-evolutionary. Many religious thinkers accept evolution as the designer's mechanism — students conflate design with young-earth creationism.

items.q: What is distinctive about the ontological argument?
items.correct: It tries to prove God's existence purely from the concept of God — a priori reasoning needing no empirical evidence about the world.
items.misconception: The ontological argument uses scientific evidence to prove God exists.
items.random_wrong: The ontological argument is the same as the cosmological argument.
items.why: Anselm's argument is famously a priori — pure reasoning from definition. Students try to fit it into evidence-based frameworks, missing what makes it uniquely controversial in philosophy.

items.q: The principle of sufficient reason is most associated with which philosopher?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Leibniz. “Hume” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In which 1714 work does Leibniz state his contingency argument?
items.why: Correct answer: Monadology. “Theodicy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year were Leibniz's Monadology and Principles of Nature and Grace written?
items.why: The real figure is 1714. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
